CURRENT AFFAIRS.

The inaugural meeting of the Catholic: Truth Society
of Ireland could scarcely be called a success. —There
were too many sermons ab it. The learned lecturer
has an over ornate and rhetorical style with which we
are utterly out of sympathy, so that we may be unduly
prejudiced with regard to the:lecture. The rev. gentle-
man who proposed the vote of thanks to his Eminence
for presiding appeared to conclude, that as he had an
audience provided for him he might as well deliver an
inaugural address of his own : he was rather on the long
" side. There was not one solitary lay voice heard on
the occasion and we think this was & serious blunder
in ‘management: it is the first occasion so"fu.r as we
. know or remember that the lay voice was entirely miss-
_ing.” The inaugural address of the Catholic Truth

Society of Ireland is held in a secular hall, and thab
fact indicates sufficiently clearly the difference' between
it and, say, a Lenten discourse. So far as our judg-
ment goes the proceedings this year so far as the in-
augural address was concerned might have been
more suitably held in a church than in a secular hall.

The note, by the way, was rather pessimistic in our
opinion : and certainly we would be sorry to think that
Treland—tame, timid, shoneen, anglicised Ireland—is
so important in the economy of the Catholic world as
was suggested in Father Kane’s address. We have no
wish to minimise Ireland nor the part she may yet play
in the Catholic world, but we are so alive to her weak-
ness and lack of grit at home that we are left uncon-
vinced by oratorical pictures of what she is destined to
do abroad. At one time we were told that we were the
finest peasantry in the world: now we are the greab
Catholic force in the world. It is pleasant to think
we are a chosen people as it was pleasant a few gene-
rations back to flatter. ourselves that we were the finest
peasantry in the world. But take care that in our
pleasant estimation of ourselves as the harvesters of
other nation’s souls that we don’t lose our own. We
want a good deal of internal spiritual and Catholic cul-
ture at home, and the security for a permanent and
normal output of missionaries abroad is conditioned by -
a state of robust and normal health at home.

Since we wrote the above notes we have heard some
whispers that throw a light on the fact that no lay voice
was heard at the inaugural proceedings. We under-
stand that laymen disappointed. Indeed there is one
very peculiar story that came to our ears. One dis-
tinguished layman who either was to speak or had been
asked to speak sent some excuse about having to be in
London or something of that sort on the night of the
proceedings. But on the night of the proceedings where
do you think this eminent gentleman was? There is
an eating club, of which the wiry ‘old Freemason, Sir
Charlie Cameron, is the leading spirit, or should we
not rather say stomach; the gourmands of this eating
club call themsélves Corinthians. They arranged for
a great tuck-in on Wednesday night, and the eminent
gentleman whose services were sought for as a speaker
to the address on ‘“ The Vocation of the Celt,’’ evi-
dently concluded that the vocation of one particular
Celt on that particular night was to have a royal tuck-in
in company with old Charlie Cameron and other cham-
pion eaters. =

Irish slates and Irish cement appear to stand for
rather hopeless branches of Irish industry. At the
Granard No. 1 District Council the.Engineer reported
that the difference in price between Irish and Welsh
slates, according to quotations, was £3 per thousand,
or £2 1s. 6d. per cottage. He reported that he had:
not yet been able to find any Irish cement. = The Engi-
neer told the Council that there was only one firm of
slate manufacturers. ~ We doubt if he is correct in
that, as we have a recollection that we unearthed a
second some years ago; but, of course, it may be
defunct since then. We believe there is only one man
or firm in the cement-making industry in Ireland. The
possibilities of the cement industry have been before
the public for years, but yet no new move, so far as
we know, has been made. < G

The Hon. Albinia Brodrick is appealing for funds.
She is sister of Viscount Midleton, and is living in the -
country near Caherdaniel in County Kerry, which may
be” best' designated by the term O'Connell’s Country.

'

A snap-shotter from one of the English ha’penny illus-
- trated dailies recently visited the place, or perhaps was
- brought to visit it; anyway he snapped lavishly, and so
a recent issue of one of these ha’penny picture papers
contained a large number of photographs portraying the
activities of the Hon. Albinia Brodrick, ‘‘ a gentle
lady of English birth,”’ as the ha’penny English pic-
ture paper styles her. = The pictures have created much
indignation, and the whole business with its appeal for
funds calls for notice by the self-respecting Irish public.
Who deputed this English woman to appeal for funds to
England through the medium partly of snapshots care-
tully arranged?

The whole- front page of the ha’penny picture paper,
and half the centre two pages, are taken up with snap-
shots concerning ““ a lady’s life of sacrifice among the
Trish peasants.”” One snap shows the Hon. Albinia
Brodrick tying a rag round a girl’s finger; it is alleged
by the paper that it represents her *dv ng a
poisoned finger.”’ We wonder what does the girl think
of being represented as being the possessor of “‘a
poisoned finger ' ?  Another snap represents an interior
of a cottage and the man of the house has a pig in his
arms just as a fashionable female might hold a puppy
dog. -~ Now men in Kerry do not fondle pigs in their
arms, but the pig is in the man’s arms right enough, so
we conclude that this < gentle lady of English birth
arranged that little detail with the-London snapper.
Another snapshot represents. her *‘ wading a brook to
visit a patient.”” - She is wading the brook certainly,
but we would like to know the name and address of the
patient she was proceeding to visit. ~There has been
a Lady Dudley nurse in the district for seven years.

There are more than two full pages of snaps evi-
dently carefully selected by Miss Brodrick.  One re-
presents her in her co-operative store selling some goods
to a young woman—we understand the young woman
customer-is her own maid! There is a picture of her
milking a cow, and we are informed on good authority
that she is squatted at the wrong side of the cow.

When the Hon. Albinia Brodrick decided to appeal
for funds she certainly arranged for a splash—over
two pages of pictures and some heavily-headed letter-
press in one issue of an English ha’penny daily picture
paper is rather a big order.  The letterpress tells us:

Camans,
Hurley Balls
Jerseys,

The Hon. Albinia Brodrick, sister of Viscount
Midleton, a former Minister of War, spepds her
days among a population of Roman:Catholics, and
she, a Protestant, has nothing but Edmlrntwn fo,l:
those hardy folk she lovingly calls” my people.’

But why represent one of ** my people fondling a pig
in his arms ? 2

Tt is stated in the picture paper that “‘she started a
little college for the teaching of Irisl} in the parish, and
there were sixty pupils.”’ Now this is absolutely in-
correct.  The college was started by the Caherdaniel
Gaelic League with the surplus funds from the local
Teis held in 1910. She was not even on the commit-
tee. We understand that Miss Brodrick attended the
college as an ordinary pupil. One of the pictures con-
sists of some ‘“mud and stone cabins with thatched
roofs,’”’ but these are part of the village of Sneem,
many miles from where ‘‘ the gentle lady of English
birth ’’ resides—almost needless to say the snapper
from London did not pick out a nice part of Sneem for
his picture. We will probably have more to say aboub
this business. :

The Dublin Trades Council, by 18 votes to 7, passed
a resolution protesting against the action of the Medical
Association trying to blackmail Approved Societies.
ackmail is an ugly word, and the fact that it was
passed at the Dublin Trades Council by 18 votes to 7
indicates the feeling that Sawbones has stirred up.

At a meeting of the Castlerea Union it was stated
that several catalogues were received. from English
firms in reference to the proposed fire escape ladder
required by the Guardians. A discussion ensued as to
whether or not there are any such ladders made in
Ireland. There was no catalogue or particulars of such
before the meeting, and the Clerk stated catalogues
from the English firms were sent without any adver-
tising at all; the firms merely saw the account of the
fire in the papers and sent them on. If there is any
firm in Ireland making these ladders, perhaps, like
some of our tanneries, it would -need a: fire on the
premises for the fact of its existence to be disclosed in
the Press.

A public meeting under the auspices of the Society:
for the Simplification of Trish Spelling was held at the
Mansion House last week.  “‘ Sacerdos,”” who
was present, writes:—‘ T am just after coming from
Canon O’Leary’s lecture in the Mansion House. It
has been the literary treat of my life. Never before
have I felt so near to and so affected by a real genuine
representative of native Irish speech and culture.
Father O’Leary’s advocacy of freedom was compelling
in its logic, and charmingly winning in its utter absence
of that bitterness which is so often associated with our
modern controversies. No matter how bilter you were
against this new departure, weéll, were you presentto-
night, and did you understand pure Irish spoken by its.
purest writer, you should have eoncluded, at least, that
the freedom-to-spell advocates were men who knew,
how to plead and argue as scholars and gentlemen.’’
Referring to .an cdtaip Peaosp as the Grand Old Man
of the Lianguage movement, he goes on:—** Well, you
should have been there to hear him. It’s impossible
to convey any adequate idea of the beautiful lecture
he gave us. The thing that strikes one most is the
vitality and vivacity of the-man. May God preserve

this dear saviour of Treland’s great language to us. =

Anything that has such a man to advocate and defend
it must be good and right.”

A correspondent draws our attention to a
letter in ‘the ‘“Irish News,”’ of Belfast, from Father

_ D. F. McCrea, P.P., of Kilrea: The letter is headed
-*“ Insulting a Parish Priest,”” and evidently has. re-
.. ference to some local incident. The part of the letter
to which our particular attention is called is: ‘‘ Since
coming to the district, practically all my employ-
ment and custom, which happened to be considerable,
“have gone_to non-Catholics; and this week has been
mostly spent in endeavouring to promote peace between

my parishioners and their Unionist neighbours. Might
I not have expected better? ”’ If practically all Father
MecCrea’s custom and the employment he gives goes to
non-Catholics we certainly are scarcely surprised if he
has -been insulted by non-Catholics. Why should
practically all of his considerable custom be kept from
Catholics?  Are the Catholics not fit to get it, or is
it ** tolerance proving ’’? =

Here is o note on bigotry in the Prison Board Ser-
vice :—The three first class governorships in the, service
ave filled by patronage appointment neither of whom
has more than ten years’ service. Two are Protestants
and one a Catholic. The only Catholic appointed to
the position of governor or deputy g.7ernor from out-
side the Service during the past thirty years. When
the governorship of Mountjoy Prison (a first class ap-
pointment) became vacant two years ago the junior
governor, with only six years’ service, was appointed
to the post, over the heads of sixteen senior men, with -
years of service ranging from ten to thirty-eight years.
He was the Catholic above mentioned. ~ When the
governorship of Belfast Prison (also a first class ap-
pointment) became vacant six months later, a junior,
with nine years’ service, was appointed to the post,
over the heads of fourteen senior men. He was a Pro-
testant. When the governorship,of Dundalk Prison
became vacant two years ago there were five deputy
governors in the service, four: Catholics and one Pro-
testant. The shortest service amongst the Catholics
was twenty-six years. The Protestant had but five
vears” service and he got the post.  There are five
deputy governors in the service to-day, three Catholics
and two Protestants. BEach of the Catholic deputy
governors has about thirty years of service, and receive
£180 each, one £140.  Neither of the two-deputy
governors who are Protestants has yet two years’ ser-
vice, and each receive as salary £200, rising by £10
annually to £300. :

We advise everyone of our readers who can possibly
do so to pay a visit to the paintings by Mr. Power
O’Malley, on view in the hall at the Gaelic League pre-
mises, 25 Rutland Square, Dublin. . Mr. O’Malley was .
born somewhere in the Ring country, Co.- Waterford,
but has spent most of his working life in the United
States. If ever there was a born painter he is one,
and notwithstanding his artistic career in America he
is an unspoiled son of Ireland with -a loving and deep
insight into Ireland. We might call him. a passionate
Trish Irelander if a man’ of his temperament may be
classified by any definition. In America he paints to
live: during his holidays in Ireland he has lived -to
paint ; and on the walls of the hall at:the Gaelic League
premises are the results of his work done in the Ri
country and in Achill. For an estimate of the artis
value of Mr. O'Malley’s pictures we refer our readers
to the arficle in anobher part of this paper-by N
ward Martyn. We confess that from ordinary picture
galleries we expect to derive little .more than a head- °
ache, but we left these pictures with regret, and feel
that we could spend days looking at them. There are
noets who: - never  write poetry, and perhaps—who
knows >—we may be painters though we never.paint!
Tor in Mr. -Martyn’s opinion Mr. O’Malley’s pictures,
like those of Manet’s, appeal chiefly to painters. They
certainly appealed tous. Mr. O’Malley does not dress
or do up like an artist: he is content to be one. :The
admission to the picture gallery is free. We recently
complained that the Irish language movement has not “
produced much .of.an outburst from the Trish speakers:

we fear modern Irish ‘‘ awt’ has been too much a
condescension from outsiders rather than a real expres-
sion from within. Mr. O’Malley is a man Irish of the
Irish. With love and insight but without sentimen-
ts.l.lty Or pose or an eye to any particular public, he has
painted Ireland because it is in him to express himself
that way. And we would almost guarantee that those
of our readers who, like ourselves, are sick of ‘“ awt "’
and the cant and twaddle and pose that are part and
parcel of the bag and baggage of that cult will appre-
ciate and respond to the paintings of Mr. O’Malley.

Last week we had something to say about the obvious
and barefaced anti-Catholic bigotry in the Sorting De-
partment of, the Dublin G.P.0O. Since then we have
found out some little information about the Telegraph
Department. In the Telegraph Department the chief
posts are held by the ‘‘ Saved,” ‘and we understand
the trail of the Mason is over the whole office, with the
result that the.staff, mainly Nationalist and Catholic,
are treated in the approved Ascendancy manner
bullied, browbeaten and misrepresented on every pos-
sible occasion, especially when it comes to the question
of promoting the best—Mason.

Is the Telegraph Department efficiently managed ?
What about the many telegraphic breaks down culmi-
nating in the deplorable mess made of the telegraphic
arrangements for the Asquith and the King Carson
meetings? The department inquiry which was
held on the telegraph muddle discovered that
the arrangements were “open to ecriticism,” but
no-doubt the fact that the muddlers were *‘ Saved
tempered even the criticism of those favoured lambs.

It would be too sweeping to say that these ‘‘ Saved "
gentlemen are entirely incompetent. Quite recently
one of those hard workers drawing £390 a year reported
a telegraph messenger for ‘‘ loitering in the office for
15 seconds.”” His economic sense must have received
a grave shock, the more so as probably the messenger
boy was an “ idolater.”” Whatever the messenger boy
was he certainly had a nice sense of humour, for he
stated in reply to the charge that he ‘‘ begged to differ
from the superintendent as the actual time spent in
loitering was nine seconds.”” TIs it any wonder that we
have telegraphic feats like that experienced in connec-
tion with the treatment of the Asquith meeting when
we have careful officials of this type controlling the
destinies of a great public department.

Hall, Bushmills, Tony Traill, LL.D., Provost of 'L'luxt‘y

Tony’s fangs are pretty well drawn by now, though he

and elsewhere. He related some case where a land-

The Registry of Deeds Office occupies an obscure,
but, nevertheless, an important position in that hetero-
geneous aggregate of public Departments—the Irish
Civil Service. It has no voice whatever in the govern-
ment.of the country, and its principal raison d’etre is
in connection with real property and money lending®
There are, however, many ‘‘ fat jobs’’ connected with
it. The chief one is what is called a political appoint-
ment, and, when vacant, is bestowed by whatever
Government is in power, on a third-rate lawyer, an
obsequious and. impecunious literary hack, or a scion
of some ancient family, at the time ““ on the rocks.”
Tstablished in the early years of the reign of Queen
Anne, with the object, we believe, of preventing

AN IRISH PAINTER.

HE exhibition of Mr. Power O'Malley's pictures

: in the Gaelic League Hall reveals before all
things the work of a born painter. These pictures are
quite devoid of literary qualities, as artists say, by which
is meant that they tell no anecdote, nor seek to illus-
trate events. They just arrest and register through
the medium of paint what the eye of the artistically
observant sees and selects. For this reason those pic-
tures will appeal chiefly to painters, as always did the
pictures of Manet, with whom M. O’Mailey
may in this - respect be compared. And re
1 wish to ensure that I may not be
understood,: and scoffed at afterwards and
to say that this was a painter as great as Munet.
I remember some years ago I was accused of having
written that O’Brien Butler was a second Wagner be-
cause I wrote that his beautiful Ceoil Side had a charm
that surpassed all modern unaccompanied voice writ-
ing except that of Wagner, a very reasonable opinion
considering the general mediocrity of modern unse-
companied music. But then commonplace pcople
whom discoveries always shock appear {o lese
right understanding when they argue about esthetics
for the purpose 'of confounding some oppuuent.
Besides apart from giving rise to such mistakes, there
is nothing that would be likely to do a young artist
more harm than such criticisms at the outset of his
career as would tend to make him ridiculous instead
of interesting. With these explanations, therefore,
and reserves, I repeat that there is a certain family
trait that links him with the greatest and most beau-
tiful handler of paint that this world has ever seen.
That is he truly, if distinctly alls Manet in the
delightful - clearness everywhere of his paint, a
cleanness which is the first mark of a colowrist. It
is impossible to look upon the beautiful paint and
colour of Dungarvan Harbour without being vaguely
reminded of Boulogne Pier. This is a rare gift and
enoygh to make M. O’Malley distinguished. — Of
the greatest qualities of Manet, however, that phan-
tasmal evanescence of tone he shows no sign of pos-
sessing yet.
Still his tone is often charming. Take for ex-
ample that half - length of a little girl in a
blue frock. What sensitiveness there is for the
delicacy of colour! = How subtle the faint pink in
the face seems an emanation from the blue dress be-
neath it. The other portraits do not please me- so
much, Some are rather “hard and -constrained; bub
this may arise from difficulty with the sitters. There
is an astonishing head of another little gitl with large
staring blue eyes and bright red cheeks, a very doll

coast scenes must chiefly claim our attention, and also
one or two farm house interiors of unwonted realisa-
tion of that charm of atmosphere and light which pro-
duce. in the observer such quiet enjoyment. T suppose
they, and indeed the rest in this show, would be called
sketches. Indeed they are sketches; but they convey
much more than the most finished work one sees in
academies or exhibitions. They are sketches in painb

catalogue), how bright the house is, haw rich the colour
*of the neglected flowers in the foreground near the
fumbled. down cabin. It may be & sketch; but there
is nothing left unaccounted for. The besb of the inte-
viors represents a girl sitting with her back to the
w and quietly knitting. There is real beauty of

met. The outstanding fact is the failure of the recent
Ulster campaign, and the strong position in which the
Home Rule Bill stands. It has been amusing during
the week to read the comments of the Tory Press.
If one were to judge from its leading articles one would
think that the Government had not a month to live,
that the Home Rule Bill was about to be withdrawn
as a hopeless fiasco, and that the King was on the eve
of sending for Mr. Bonar Law to form a new Ministry.
How wide of the actual facts all this is need not be
stated. The unreality that lies behind the assumed
confidence of the Tories was never more apparent than
at present, and one is tempted to laugh at the absurd
way in which they try to infuse a little spirit into their
cause, and a little courage into their followers. But
if they have no better grounds to go upon than the
foolish vapourings and meaningless boasts of their
Press this last week, the Tory Party must be in as
bad a way as its worst enemy could wish to see it.
The Ulster campaign has failed dismally in its great
purpose, which was to frighten and intimidate the
sh public into the belief that, if the Government’s
programme is persisted in, there will be civil war in
the North of Ireland. The people throughout Ireland
itself know what a delightful farce that pretence is.

The chief source of satisfaction in the position from
the Trish point of view, apart from the progress of the
l_E[ome Rule Bill itself, is the fact that the Government
is determined that it is not going to allow the Tories
to force another general election. The whole energies
of thg Opposition are to be concentrated on bringing an
election about.  They are like the proverbial gambler,
who has lost his money, and who wants another throw
of the dice in the hope that his luck will change.  But
with a majority against them in the House of Commons
of 108, and with a House of Lords that can no longer
““ do the needful ** for them, it is hard to see how they
are going to succeed in their designs. It is, of course,
always possible for the' Upper Chamber to throw out
the Army Annual Bill, or the Expiring Laws Continu-
ance Bill, but I have not heard anyone seriously sug-
gest that it is likely to adopt a course of this kind which
might not, after all, bring about an election, but which

“would certainly bring about the ears of the Tories the

indignation of -the public. Therefore, the outlook is
satisfactory. In politics it is never safe to adopt the
7éle of prophet, as pcor William O’Brien has shown us
so often of recent years, but it is not venturing upon
that dangerous occupation to say that so far as the
future is concerned the political prospects are most
encouraging. RrcHARD HAZLETON.

This is but one form of intellectual activity in which
English Catholics seem to put us to shame. I could
easily indicate others.

I will not try to solve the vexing problem or to place
the blame. It is a pleasanter thing to point to signs
- of the righting of the unequal balance. And happily

one does occasionally see such signs. A book which I
have lately been reading and which I desire to intro-
duce to readers of the Lraprr, has, I think, an impor-
tant significance in this direction.  ‘‘ Searching the
Scriptures,”’ by the Rev. T. P. Gallagher, is a closely
-printed volume of some 500 pages on a subject of per-
ennial interest to every Catholic, and it has been
written not by a University or college professor, but by
a priest on the mission in an Irish country pavish. Two
things must at once strike even the casual reader of
this book: the wonderful familiarity which the author
shows with all the modern literature, whether written
from a Christian or a Rationalistic standpoint, that has
grown around the subject of his study; and the clear,
vigorous, sometimes trenchant style in which he ex-
presses the views resulting from his immense reading
and his ripe thought. This is no dry-as-dust study
written in a dry-as-dust way: if Father Gellagher has
assimilated a very library of learning, he has had his
eye on the needs of the day, and by his manner of
writing he has made the popular reader of intelligence
free of his treasures.

The subject of the book is one which has always had
an irresistible fascination for unbeliever and believer
alike. A casual glance at any considerable bock-stall

THE LEADER. , : 241

will discover more than one popular work at a popular
price dealing with the career and character of Jesus of
Nazareth. These books are written from all sorts of
points of view: here, perhaps, we have a Catholic
““ Life of Jesus, the Son of God,”" and side by side with
it a rationalistic study of ‘‘ Jesus the Carpenter of
Nazareth.” And the very popularity of the subject
and this strange diversity of treatment show the neces-
sity and the seasonableness of the handbook provided
by Father Gallagher. The title of his work, ‘“ Search-
ing the Scriptures,’ is suggested by the words of Christ
Himself, ‘‘ Search the Scriptures: and the same are
they that give testimony of me.”” The learned author
searches the Scriptuves referred to—the Old Testa-
ment Books—from beginning to end, and makes them
render up their testimony to the Expected of Nations.
He takes one by one the prophetic passages referring
to Christ in these Sacred Books, and leaving altogether
aside the inspired character of the narratives and con-
sidering them merely as historical documents, he asks

-himself whether the appeal of Jesus of Nazareth is

justified. Bit by bit he pieces together, with illumin-
ating comment, the picture of the future Messias
sketched by many hands through many a hundred
years, from Moses to Malachy. And then having
placed the finished picture in all its majestic propor-
tions and enthralling beauty before the reader, he turns
to the grand historic Figure that lives in the pages of
the New Testament. With dramatic swiftness he
compares the picture with the Man who claims to be
represented by it: and as the reader concludes the last
